title: When DNSSEC Goes Wrong: How Cloudflare Responded to the .de TLD Outage
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-07, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.cloudflare.com%2Fde-tld-outage-dnssec%2F%3Futm_source=tldrit/1/0100019e025acca8-580ae253-dc10-4761-88cd-cbedf3f4f931-000000/HEGds2e0oYL1ScVFwJaoENuCV1ORkVV3pcYhl_fOREA=452
summary: When DNSSEC Goes Wrong: How Cloudflare Responded to the .de TLD Outage (4 minute read) A misconfiguration by DENIC caused invalid DNSSEC signatures for the .de TLD, leading validating resolvers like Cloudflare to return widespread SERVFAIL errors. The response required temporarily disabling DNSSEC validation for .de, underscoring how integrity-first systems can create global availability failures when things break upstream.

summary: Pre-renewal lifecycle flow (3 minute read) The 90 days before renewal should not be treated like a normal campaign window. summary: How Google made their Gemma LLM 3x faster (4 minute read) Google's Gemma model achieved a three-fold increase in inference speed using speculative decoding. This technique involves a tiny, fast 'Drafter' predicting tokens that the main model verifies in parallel to efficiently use the GPU and save memory.

title: OpenAI's WebRTC Problem
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-08, Reading time: 13 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fmoq.dev%2Fblog%2Fwebrtc-is-the-problem%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e075f7305-e0a9c9a2-ddfa-4247-b293-d1117cdfab7d-000000/aQ0te8j2d2Is0odtduayTldmvIkdZfVlJJOR95iQsrg=452
summary: OpenAI's WebRTC Problem (13 minute read) WebRTC is a poor choice for voice-based AI due to its packet-dropping design, which favors low latency over the audio accuracy needed for reliable prompts, and introduces technical debt with complex port management. The better alternative is QUIC, which uses unique connection IDs for easy network switching and load balancing.

title: The Architecture Of Local-First Web Development

summary: The Architecture Of Local-First Web Development (33 minute read) Local-first development is a paradigm shift where application data is primarily stored on the user's device instead of a central server. Modern implementations often use SQLite compiled to WebAssembly to turn the browser into a high-performance database node, managing complexities like conflict resolution and data migration through specialized sync engines.

title: AI applications have two growth curves

Slow growth is required to build companies that competitors can't easily copy in a quarter when the funding cycle turns.

title: Don't sell the build effort

summary: Don't sell the build effort (6 minute read) Avoid pricing software based on build effort, as it disconnects from optimal value-based pricing. summary: The Six-Hour Codex Run That Survived a Five-Hour Pause (10 minute read) /goal is a headline feature that shipped in Codex on April 30 that introduced persisted goals, goal states that survive terminal restarts, laptop sleeps, and multi-hour passes without re-prompting. The feature injects a developer message on resume rather than waiting for the user to time anything. It allows users to turn off their devices and pick up exactly where they left off without re-prompting anything.

title: Good QC for RL Data
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-08, Reading time: 18 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.seancai.com%2Fphilosophy%2Fgood_qc_rl_data%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019e07bf44ef-39419a35-9181-4a26-8037-e18c56ae738c-000000/Xm9JARloQ4fXoPliugwagD45aP3dsATfHuj4HxyZxFE=452
summary: Good QC for RL Data (18 minute read) There needs to be a higher bar for what good quality looks like. Any vendor selling into a frontier lab is being judged implicitly during the purchase decision, and most are failing multiple quality control gates at once. Standardizing QC for evaluating how well data tests something on the performance-cost-latency Pareto curve is of the utmost importance. The vendors who don't internalize a higher QC bar will start running into problems this year.

title: Long AI Short AGI
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-08, Reading time: 3 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2F1984.substack.com%2Fp%2Flong-ai-short-agi%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019e07bf44ef-39419a35-9181-4a26-8037-e18c56ae738c-000000/I8-IYdPMltNd8tjxqdpFZlaDhchBxR2R4GJybuiBK4Y=452
summary: Long AI Short AGI (3 minute read) Silicon Valley's narrative emphasizes AGI as the ultimate scarce resource, but the rapid commoditization of AI models challenges this. Intelligence now follows the same path as compute, bandwidth, and storage, where market forces drive competition and reduce costs. The real winners in AI won't necessarily have superior models but will own customer relationships and proprietary data, much like past tech giants.

summary: When the Uncertainty Is Bigger Than the Shock: Scenario Modelling for English Local Elections (13 minute read) Scenario models are data products, not predictions. Version assumptions, freeze model artifacts, store residuals, expose uncertainty bands, log guardrails, and plan post-event audits.

title: How Discord Automates ScyllaDB Clusters at Scale
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-11, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fdiscord.com%2Fblog%2Fhow-discord-automates-scylladb-clusters-at-scale%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e1683f14c-18301f7b-7750-4b06-bb4c-5b9efe951dae-000000/d0Sdy5bKC813HHnEFG8mp7ptUmvN8d3i-dRbmYn5NWQ=452
summary: How Discord Automates ScyllaDB Clusters at Scale (6 minute read) Discord built Scylla Control Plane (SCP), a robust automation framework in Rust, to manage complex operational tasks across its large fleet of ScyllaDB clusters. SCP uses declarative YAML workflows composed of idempotent tasks and explicit safety conditions, with configurable parallelism and zoning constraints, enabling safe operations like rolling restarts, cluster expansions, and standing up shadow clusters in under two hours.

title: Enhancing Flink Deployment with Shadow Testing
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-11, Reading time: 3 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fengineering.grab.com%2Fenchancing-flink-shadow-testing%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e1683f14c-18301f7b-7750-4b06-bb4c-5b9efe951dae-000000/0cJeVAwnP464F-AmCi3ZzBCeMMlBIpVdNthd8oh6mZE=452
summary: Enhancing Flink Deployment with Shadow Testing (3 minute read) Grab's data streaming team added a Shadow Testing stage to its Apache Flink deployment pipeline to eliminate the ~10-minute rollback downtime caused by production-only failures. A shadow job runs in parallel with the main job in production, using distinct consumer groups and sink destinations to avoid interference while comparing metrics and outputs. The approach increases deployment frequency and reduces change failure rate.

title: How BigQuery actually executes a query
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-11, Reading time: 10 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2Fnkq4z6/1/0100019e1683f14c-18301f7b-7750-4b06-bb4c-5b9efe951dae-000000/-PO3vWMypdURU5bhskH9OlhCVLwrpxOZHpXGuxNvk9U=452
summary: How BigQuery actually executes a query (and why most optimization advice misses half the picture) (10 minute read) BigQuery performance comes down to understanding its execution model: queries run as parallel stages across slots, and the main hidden cost is shuffle, not just bytes scanned. The Execution Details panel reveals stage-level slot-ms, max vs average compute time, and join strategy, making it possible to spot skew, fan-out, and expensive hash joins.

summary: You Need AI That Reduces Maintenance Costs (7 minute read) You have to manage maintenance costs if you want a productive team. AI may help teams produce code faster, but maintenance costs can build up exponentially if teams don't manage the code properly. Even if AI produces code that's just as easy to maintain as human-written code, the productivity gains don't last. Chase improvements to coding speed, but spend just as much time chasing improvements to maintenance costs as well.

summary: Learning on the Shop floor (10 minute read) River is an AI agent that lives in Shopify's company Slack. The agent's success comes from Shopify's employees watching each other interact with it.

summary: Projecting React (18 minute read) The React API was rebuilt from scratch for only TanStack Start, resulting in a ~9KB gzip size (compared to React's ~60KB) and running 2–3 times faster while passing all tests and powering two production sites. Cheap code regeneration makes shipping full general-purpose dependencies obsolete, suggesting that future web development will be like Linux distributions, where consumers own shape-specific projections of libraries.

title: How lakebase architecture delivers 5x faster Postgres writes
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-11, Reading time: 10 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.databricks.com%2Fblog%2Fhow-lakebase-architecture-delivers-5x-faster-postgres-writes%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e16d2d503-fe346bb1-3eb1-41e3-8e3d-2d4d9e6d3b64-000000/VysCnhEJhbSnZ5zrrIsTpEZY4oxOeYj-pJOsPbHofgM=452
summary: How lakebase architecture delivers 5x faster Postgres writes (10 minute read) Lakebase architecture by Databricks achieves a five-fold increase in Postgres write speed by separating compute and storage layers, which eliminates the "Full Page Write" bottleneck and offloads tasks like crash-recovery. These global optimizations reduce log traffic by 94% and improve read speeds by half.

title: The unwritten laws of software engineering
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-11, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fnewsletter.manager.dev%2Fp%2Fthe-unwritten-laws-of-software-engineering%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e16d2d503-fe346bb1-3eb1-41e3-8e3d-2d4d9e6d3b64-000000/96wVxx6eyDjyjybxI8l_ZIkW884WJzh_8xhYH--04Js=452
summary: The unwritten laws of software engineering (8 minute read) Hard-won software engineering rules tell us that when production fails, you must roll back before debugging and treat all untested recovery plans as fictional. Developers should expect external dependencies to fail, apply a "four eyes" rule for risky changes, and understand that temporary fixes often become permanent.

title: Idempotency Is Easy Until the Second Request Is Different
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-11, Reading time: 25 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.dochia.dev%2Fblog%2Fidempotency%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e16d2d503-fe346bb1-3eb1-41e3-8e3d-2d4d9e6d3b64-000000/JMdVIrBEj7rPC015PW_JJc_Ea6Rr-mjbdr7kJ0VP3eQ=452
summary: Idempotency Is Easy Until the Second Request Is Different (25 minute read) True idempotency goes beyond replaying responses and must address complex issues like concurrent requests, partial failures, and state mismatches. To maintain consistency and prevent duplicate actions, the server must use atomic operations, hash the validated command, and differentiate between genuine retries and conflicting commands using the same key.

summary: Meta U-turns on encryption push for Instagram as DMs go plaintext (2 minute read) Meta removed the option for end-to-end encrypted Instagram DMs after low opt-in, steering users to WhatsApp instead. Meta already uses private AI interactions for ad targeting, and has not ruled out similar use of Instagram messages.

title: Security in a Post-Mythos World

summary: Security in a Post-Mythos World (6 minute read) Powerful tools such as Anthropic's Mythos will allow security researchers to scale up their operations to uncover new vulnerabilities while working in tandem with AI. Threat modeling is an area where AI tooling excels, and security teams should leverage it.

title: Useful memories become faulty when continuously updated by LLMs
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-11, Reading time: 30 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fdylanzsz.github.io%2Ffaulty-memory%2F%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019e1735d930-18647a31-cbb4-4831-a8e5-0b224ae47c14-000000/yuxHdjJ8Y7WzuFobx5jYMERm4ZV_MBb5XtG64lnYO3c=452
summary: Useful memories become faulty when continuously updated by LLMs (30 minute read) Agent updates don't always make memories more useful. They can actually make agents perform worse than the same model with no memory at all. The failure is in the rewrite step. Until agents can decide when and how to consolidate, the safest default is to keep episodic memory and abstract sparingly, or not at all.

Learn why a vertically-integrated stack is essential to eliminate vendor-on-vendor lag, ensure architectural integrity, and truly own your user's trust by verifying the tech behind your verifier. Read the blog here.

title: Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-12, Reading time: 15 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fleehanchung.github.io%2Fblogs%2F2026%2F05%2F08%2Fhidden-technical-debt-agent-harness%2F%3Futm_source=tldrproduct/1/0100019e1ba72db4-5ef01191-ff4a-42bd-a89f-a90b482617f7-000000/Lu1gUP1EOBNzN52wsjGhjk_Sg3yN4uLMYD-TusI02es=452
summary: Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness (15 minute read) Agent harnesses are hidden AI technical debt. As models improve, today's prompts, workflows, and tool wrappers will become obsolete, so teams should keep harnesses thin, invest in durable skills and evals, and build systems they can replace easily.

title: The computers talk to us now
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-12, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Froundup.getdbt.com%2Fp%2Fthe-computers-talk-to-us-now%3Futm_source=tldrproduct/1/0100019e1ba72db4-5ef01191-ff4a-42bd-a89f-a90b482617f7-000000/CXTys5PI2WnaiZ6WM-plcYlqE827aVz9hlVqTGpD6KU=452
summary: The computers talk to us now (4 minute read) Computers can now talk to us and do real skilled work. Software, teams, and organizations must adapt to a world where agents are no longer just tools, but active participants in how systems get built and used.

title: Escape from agentic loop
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-12, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.proofofconcept.pub%2Fp%2Fescape-from-agentic-loop%3Futm_source=tldrproduct/1/0100019e1ba72db4-5ef01191-ff4a-42bd-a89f-a90b482617f7-000000/yAgY1MiGzZL5V4zEbbStcusOQmQOGhOHrmcogDKgbfk=452
summary: Escape from agentic loop (5 minute read) AI agent orchestration can become “doom building,” draining attention while feeling productive. Most agent work should be supervised through clear scopes and review blocks, while staying deeply involved only in the creative work that needs human judgment.

title: Minimally Viable Consistency
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-12, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fcutlefish.substack.com%2Fp%2Ftbm-421-minimally-viable-consistency%3Futm_source=tldrproduct/1/0100019e1ba72db4-5ef01191-ff4a-42bd-a89f-a90b482617f7-000000/nCzEwdEOqjaYfjy--ZQu7E1TAzRSZ0AkGJ29BZRV_HM=452
summary: Minimally Viable Consistency (5 minute read) Organizations need enough consistency to stay aligned, but not so much that teams lose flexibility. AI can help translate across different ways of working, as long as it does not replace the human sensemaking that creates real alignment.
